expectations.text: ponder.config.ts reads deployedContracts from SE-2 nextjs package
expectations.evidence: In packages/ponder/ponder.config.ts line 2: `import deployedContracts from "../nextjs/contracts/deployedContracts";` — the config imports and uses deployedContracts to build the contracts object dynamically.

expectations.text: ponder.config.ts reads scaffoldConfig for network detection
expectations.evidence: In packages/ponder/ponder.config.ts line 3: `import scaffoldConfig from "../nextjs/scaffold.config";` — then line 5: `const targetNetwork = scaffoldConfig.targetNetworks[0];` uses it to determine the target chain.

expectations.text: Package named @se-2/ponder following SE-2 workspace convention
expectations.evidence: In packages/ponder/package.json line 2: `"name": "@se-2/ponder"` — matches the SE-2 workspace naming convention used by @se-2/hardhat and @se-2/nextjs.

expectations.text: Uses Ponder virtual module imports (ponder:registry, ponder:schema, ponder:api)
expectations.evidence: src/YourContract.ts uses `import { ponder } from "ponder:registry"` and `import { greetingChange } from "ponder:schema"`. src/api/index.ts uses `import { db } from "ponder:api"` and `import schema from "ponder:schema"`. All three virtual modules are used.

expectations.text: Schema uses onchainTable (not older createSchema API)
expectations.evidence: In ponder.schema.ts line 1: `import { onchainTable } from "ponder";` and line 3: `export const greetingChange = onchainTable("greeting_change", (t) => ({...}));` — uses the modern onchainTable API.
In src/YourContract.ts line 4: `ponder.on("YourContract:GreetingChange", async ({ event, context }) => {` — uses the correct 'ContractName:EventName' colon-separated format.
In src/YourContract.ts lines 5-13: `await context.db.insert(greetingChange).values({ id: event.id, greetingSetter: event.args.greetingSetter, ... });` — uses the correct insert/values pattern.

expectations.text: Hono-based API setup for GraphQL (not old express-style)
expectations.evidence: In src/api/index.ts: `import { Hono } from "hono";` then `const app = new Hono();` and `app.use("/graphql", graphql({ db, schema }));` with `export default app;` — uses Hono framework, not express.

expectations.text: Root package.json has ponder proxy scripts
expectations.evidence: Root package.json contains: `"ponder:dev": "yarn workspace @se-2/ponder dev"`, `"ponder:start": "yarn workspace @se-2/ponder start"`, `"ponder:codegen": "yarn workspace @se-2/ponder codegen"`, `"ponder:serve": "yarn workspace @se-2/ponder serve"`, `"ponder:lint": "yarn workspace @se-2/ponder lint"`, `"ponder:typecheck": "yarn workspace @se-2/ponder typecheck"` — all six proxy scripts present.
packages/ponder/ponder-env.d.ts exists with 22 lines declaring modules for `ponder:registry`, `ponder:schema`, and `ponder:api` with proper Virtual type imports from ponder.

claims.claim: The executor read the SKILL.md file before implementing
claims.evidence: Transcript line 3 shows Read tool call for .agents/skills/ponder/SKILL.md, and line 5 shows the full SKILL.md content was returned.

claims.claim: A frontend events page was created at packages/nextjs/app/events/page.tsx
claims.evidence: The file exists in the worktree at packages/nextjs/app/events/page.tsx and was copied to outputs as nextjs--app--events--page.tsx.

claims.claim: The Header.tsx was modified to add an Events navigation link
claims.evidence: Transcript lines 111-116 show Edit tool calls adding MagnifyingGlassIcon import and Events menu link. The final Header.tsx (read back in transcript lines 126) confirms the Events link at line 28-32.

claims.claim: packages/ponder was added to root workspaces array
claims.evidence: Root package.json workspaces.packages includes "packages/ponder" alongside hardhat and nextjs.

claims.claim: graphql and graphql-request dependencies were added to nextjs package
claims.evidence: In nextjs package.json lines 29-30: `"graphql": "^16.9.0"` and `"graphql-request": "^7.1.0"` are present in dependencies.

suggestions.reason: No assertion checks whether the ponder.config.ts content is genuinely adapted to the project's contracts versus just being copy-pasted from the SKILL.md template. In this case the implementation appears to be a verbatim copy of the skill's reference implementation, which happens to be correct but doesn't demonstrate adaptation. Consider adding an assertion that checks the schema fields match the actual GreetingChange event signature from the deployed contract.

No assertion checks the frontend events page content or whether it actually queries the Ponder GraphQL API. The events page is a significant deliverable but no expectation validates its existence or quality.

eval_feedback.overall: The assertions cover the Ponder package structure comprehensively. The main gap is no validation of the frontend integration (events page, Header modification) which represents a significant portion of the implementation work.
